identity.mission: Dopamine Loop exists to explore the hidden psychological costs of modern life, success, loneliness, identity, and ambition through cinematic storytelling, celebrity interviews, movie scenes, and philosophical narration. The channel helps viewers question who they are, why they feel empty, and what it truly means to build a meaningful life in a world obsessed with attention, status, and constant stimulation.
identity.audience: Dopamine Loop is built for teenagers and young adults, especially men between 16 and 35, who feel disconnected, ambitious, lonely, misunderstood, or obsessed with becoming more than they currently are. The audience is drawn to self-improvement, masculinity, psychology, philosophy, internet culture, success, discipline, celebrities, and emotionally intense content that feels deep, cinematic, and brutally honest.
identity.forbidden: politics, religion, dating advice, fitness content
identity.refs: Alastair, Motiversity, Aperture, Pursuit of Wonder

script.tone:
The writing tone should be deeply philosophical, emotionally intense, cinematic, and highly descriptive. Scripts should feel like a mix of a late-night monologue, a psychological documentary, a motivational speech, and an internal conversation someone has with themselves at 2 AM.

The narration should be written in short, powerful sentences that are easy to understand but emotionally heavy. It should constantly create curiosity, tension, and reflection by revealing uncomfortable truths, asking thought-provoking questions, and using strong metaphors, contradictions, and emotional observations.

Every script should feel personal and human rather than robotic or overly academic. It should sound like someone exposing hidden truths about loneliness, ambition, identity, success, masculinity, self-worth, or modern culture in a way that feels relatable and unforgettable.

The tone should frequently shift between:

Calm and reflective
Dark and unsettling
Motivational and intense
Emotional and vulnerable
Mysterious and curiosity-driven

The scripts should be very detailed, visually descriptive, and designed to naturally support fast-paced editing, motion graphics, interview clips, subtitles, zooms, overlays, and Remotion scene changes.

Key writing characteristics:

Use paradoxes and contradictions
Use strong emotional hooks in the first 15 seconds
Use short impactful lines
Use pauses and dramatic transitions
Use vivid imagery and visual metaphors
Use rhetorical questions
Use quotable lines throughout
Make the viewer feel seen, understood, and emotionally connected
End with a deep philosophical conclusion that lingers in the viewer’s mind
script.hook: Start with the most powerful thing the celebrity ever said. No context. No intro. Just them saying it with their face first then pan to b-roll
script.celebrities: Kanye West, Steve Jobs, Jim Carrey, Kobe Bryant, David Goggins, Tyler the Creator, Conor McGregor, Johnny Depp, Robert Downey Jr, Denzel Washington, John Cena, Mike Tyson, Volodymyr Zelenskyy, Elon Musk, Jeff Bezos, Mark Zuckerberg, Bill Gates, Steve Harvey, Dua Lipa, Olivia Rodrigo, Harry Styles, Zendaya, Timothée Chalamet, Leonardo DiCaprio, Brad Pitt,Angelina Jolie, Tom Cruise, Jennifer Lawrence, Margot Robbie, Ryan Reynolds, Dwayne Johnson, Chris Hemsworth, Scarlett Johansson, Robert Downey Jr., Emma Stone, Will Smith, Keanu Reeves, Johnny Depp, Tom Holland, Millie Bobby Brown, Sydney Sweeney, Jenna Ortega, Kim Kardashian, Kylie Jenner, Kendall Jenner, Oprah Winfrey, Ellen DeGeneres, Jimmy Fallon, MrBeast, PewDiePie, Logan Paul, KSI, Charli D'Amelio, Addison Rae, Khaby Lame, Cristiano Ronaldo, Lionel Messi, Neymar, Kylian Mbappé, Erling Haaland, LeBron James, Stephen Curry, Michael Jordan, Serena Williams, Novak Djokovic, Roger Federer, Lewis Hamilton, Max Verstappen, Tiger Woods, Usain Bolt
script.lesson: Always ends with a single philosophical question or statement the viewer takes with them from the celebrity. Never preachy. Just true.
script.forbidden_words: amazing, journey, grind, hustle, level up, boss up, sigma, alpha
script.power_words: obsession, silence, pressure, cost, truth, weight, nothing, everything

visuals.vis_palette: Black, amber, deep red, cold blue, dark purple
visuals.vis_accent_color: White for quotes, yellow for emphasis with a mix of fonts like broadway, caramel, American typewritter and bookman depending on the mood and emphasis of words
visuals.vis_broll_rules:
trapped/cage → prison corridor or locked room
failing → slow motion fall or crumbling structure
rising → time-lapse city at night, rocket launch, single figure climbing
lonely → empty stadium, person alone in crowd, dark city street
money/success → penthouse view, luxury car at night, stacked papers
time → antique clock, sand falling, calendar burning
fear → narrow hallway, shadow, flickering light
fire/passion → candle in dark room, furnace, distant fire on horizon
* discipline → alarm clock at 5 AM, person running before sunrise, hands taping wrists, gym lights turning on, empty basketball court
* obsession → person repeating same action, dozens of sticky notes on wall, messy desk, late-night laptop glow, close-up of tired eyes
* ambition → skyscrapers, private jet runway, businessman alone in office, city skyline from rooftop, athlete staring at field before game
* regret → old photographs, empty room, packed boxes, unread messages, train leaving station
* jealousy → watching someone else celebrate, scrolling social media alone, blurred figures laughing in background
* heartbreak → empty side of bed, rain on window, person deleting photos, walking alone at night
* betrayal → handshake turning cold, shadow leaving doorway, broken glass, text messages ignored
* anxiety → shaking hands, crowded subway, fast cuts of clocks, heavy breathing, flickering fluorescent lights
* depression → messy room, curtains closed, person sitting on floor, TV static, slow rain on window
* confidence → walking through crowd in slow motion, boxer entering ring, person adjusting suit, spotlight turning on
* masculinity → weightlifting, boxing, working with hands, cold showers, outdoor training, construction sites
* femininity → mirror reflections, soft light through curtains, makeup removed, emotional close-ups, slow-motion dancing
* ego → giant stage, applause, mirrors, luxury watches, person looking down at city skyline
* validation → social media notifications, crowd cheering, selfie flashes, likes increasing on screen
* burnout → messy coffee cups, tired eyes, laptop open at 3 AM, person asleep at desk
* addiction → endless scrolling, slot machines, repeated habits, neon lights, fast food wrappers, empty bottles
* social media → rapid app scrolling, notifications exploding, ring lights, influencer lifestyle clips, crowded feeds
* identity → person looking in mirror, changing outfits, masks, passport photos, blurred reflections
* self-worth → child looking at trophy, person staring at old awards, hands holding rejection letter
* isolation → single light in apartment window, person eating alone, empty train station, snowy street at night
* chaos → traffic, crowds, screaming news clips, market crashes, flashing headlines, storm footage
* peace → ocean waves, mountains, sunrise through trees, candle burning slowly, person sitting quietly
* healing → journaling, sunrise walk, person cleaning room, opening curtains, watering plants
* revenge → intense training montage, person silently working late, close-up of focused eyes, storm clouds
* love → holding hands, dancing in kitchen, old home videos, slow-motion smiles
* grief → funeral flowers, empty chair, black clothing, person standing alone at grave
* temptation → neon lights, expensive watches, luxury stores, text messages popping up, casino floors
* hope → sunrise over city, child running, rocket launch, person opening door into bright light
* freedom → motorcycle on empty road, plane taking off, ocean cliff, open field, bird flying away
* routine → making coffee, brushing teeth, tying shoes, train commute, opening laptop every morning
* boredom → blank stare, empty classroom, repetitive office work, TV channels changing
* rebellion → graffiti, motorcycles, ripped posters, protests, black clothing, speeding cars
* childhood → playgrounds, VHS tapes, birthday candles, school hallways, old toys
* nostalgia → cassette tapes, CRT televisions, arcade machines, old photographs, Polaroids
* success → trophy case, champagne, luxury apartment, interview flashes, signing contracts
* failure → missed basketball shot, broken trophy, unfinished notebook, empty bank account screen
* creativity → sketchbooks, editing timeline, studio lights, piano keys, paint on hands
* spirituality → church candles, praying hands, stained glass windows, sunrise on mountain
* sports intensity → boxer in corner before fight, football locker room, sprinter at starting line, basketball free throw in silence
* competition → race finish line, chess match, poker face, athlete staring down opponent
* resilience → person getting up after falling, bruised hands, runner in rain, cracked mirror still standing
* exhaustion → sweat dripping, athlete collapsed on bench, yawning at desk, heavy footsteps
* temptation vs discipline → split screen between party and early morning gym, junk food vs meal prep, sleeping in vs alarm clock
* inner war → mirror reflection arguing, split-screen duplicate of same person, shadow version following protagonist
* modern emptiness → luxury apartment with no one inside, person scrolling phone in silence, crowded room where no one speaks
meaning → person staring at stars, journal pages, walking through forest, sitting alone at sunrise
visuals.vis_forbidden: * Avoid obvious AI-generated faces, hands, crowds, or environments * Avoid generic smiling stock footage that feels fake or corporate * Avoid bright, overly saturated colors unless used intentionally for contrast * Avoid low-quality clips from small creators with poor lighting, shaky cameras, or weak composition * Avoid TikTok-style meme edits unless they serve a specific emotional point * Avoid overused motivational stock footage like random businessmen shaking hands * Avoid fake luxury footage that looks cheap or unrealistic * Avoid cartoonish animations or childish icons * Avoid clean, sterile corporate office footage unless used ironically * Avoid empty drone shots with no emotional meaning * Avoid overly polished “influencer lifestyle” footage that feels fake * Avoid excessive neon colors unless the scene is about temptation, nightlife, or overstimulation * Avoid happy family stock footage unless the scene specifically discusses family, childhood, or loss * Avoid random cinematic clips that do not support the narration * Avoid overusing one visual style for too long; scenes should constantly evolve * Avoid static shots that remain unchanged for more than a few seconds * Avoid cheesy text animations or PowerPoint-style transitions * Avoid royalty-free footage that obviously looks staged * Avoid inspirational footage of people high-fiving, laughing in offices, or walking in slow motion with coffee unless it has a darker twist * Avoid overusing glitch effects to the point they become distracting * Avoid meme sound effects or comedic editing that breaks immersion * Avoid clean white backgrounds unless making a deliberate contrast moment * Avoid showing success as only money, cars, and watches—always balance it with loneliness, pressure, or emptiness * Avoid perfect-looking people with unrealistic lifestyles unless the point is to criticize modern image culture * Avoid clips where celebrities look overly posed or rehearsed; prefer emotional, candid, uncomfortable, or intense moments * Avoid using the same celebrity too often in one video unless they are the main protagonist * Avoid slow pacing without visual movement, subtitles, zooms, overlays, or scene changes * Avoid repetitive b-roll categories; mix interviews, movie scenes, city shots, symbols, objects, and emotional close-ups * Avoid visuals that feel too “YouTube guru” or “hustle culture” unless the goal is to critique them * Avoid obvious green screen footage or fake studio backgrounds * Avoid bright daytime footage unless the emotional tone supports hope, nostalgia, or peace * Avoid excessive use of smiling group shots because they weaken the lonely, philosophical atmosphere * Avoid overusing luxury imagery without emotional context * Avoid visuals that feel too safe, generic, or predictable * Every visual should either:    * deepen the emotion   * reinforce the message   * create contrast   * build curiosity   * symbolize the internal struggle of the scene
visuals.vis_aroll_rule: Celebrity on screen only during their most impactful direct statements — usually 3 seconds or less. All longer explanations and context play over B-roll. Celebrity is heard but not always seen.

mograph.triggers:
* trapped = cinematic rusted prison bars slide inward toward the camera with shallow depth of field, cold blue lighting, dust particles floating in the air, dramatic shadows across concrete walls
* drowning = dark water slowly rises over the screen from below with realistic ripples, reflections, bubbles, and distant muffled light fading away overhead
* rising = glowing ash particles and smoke trails spiral upward into a beam of light above a dark city skyline at night
* lonely = single chair in the center of a massive empty room under one dim spotlight, with cinematic fog and long shadows stretching across the floor
* time = giant antique clock suspended in darkness, gears turning in slow motion as pieces break apart and dissolve into floating dust
* cost = massive stack of gold coins collapsing in slow motion across a black reflective floor with dramatic sparks and metallic debris
* fear = realistic cracked glass spreading from the center of the screen toward the edges while a dark silhouette slowly approaches behind it
* pressure = concrete walls slowly moving inward in a narrow hallway with flickering overhead lights, dust falling from the ceiling, and visible cracks
* fire = glowing orange embers drifting through a dark smoky room with subtle flames in the distance and cinematic heat distortion
* void = thousands of black particles falling endlessly into a giant dark abyss with low fog and faint blue light in the background
* obsession = hundreds of red strings stretching across a dark room, connecting walls, photos, clocks, and objects while tightening toward the center
* regret = old Polaroid photos floating in slow motion while the edges burn away into ash against a black background
* success = luxury penthouse skyline view at night with city lights below, champagne glasses, expensive watch on table, but empty room and cold atmosphere
* failure = cracked trophy falling in slow motion onto concrete floor, exploding into pieces with dust and sparks
* anxiety = giant floating smartphone notifications multiplying rapidly around the camera until they fill the entire screen and blur together
* burnout = flickering fluorescent office lights above an empty desk covered in coffee cups, papers, and glowing laptop screens
* heartbreak = realistic glass heart suspended in darkness, slowly cracking apart with glowing red light inside
* ego = giant mirror reflection of a face slowly fracturing while camera pushes closer into the cracks
* fame = endless paparazzi camera flashes in darkness, overexposing the frame while celebrity silhouette stands alone in center
* identity = multiple realistic faces glitching and morphing into each other with distorted reflections and broken mirrors
* addiction = infinite vertical scroll of social media posts, neon lights, notifications, and blurred faces moving endlessly downward
* jealousy = blurred figure celebrating victory in the distance while foreground character stands in shadow watching silently
* isolation = elevator doors slowly closing on a single person standing under harsh white light in an otherwise dark hallway
* freedom = heavy metal chains snapping apart in extreme slow motion, pieces flying through the air with sparks and dust
* hope = first rays of sunrise breaking through dark storm clouds above a city skyline with warm light slowly filling the frame
* resilience = bruised person standing back up from the ground in heavy rain with dramatic backlighting and slow-motion water droplets
* revenge = single match being struck in total darkness, flame reflecting in focused eyes
* nostalgia = old VHS tape unraveling across a dusty wooden floor surrounded by old CRT televisions and static screens
* childhood = abandoned carousel spinning slowly at night in an empty amusement park with fog and flickering lights
* purpose = long dark tunnel with a distant bright light at the end while camera slowly pushes forward
* death = single candle extinguishing in darkness with smoke trail rising slowly into black background
* self-doubt = giant handwritten phrases like “NOT ENOUGH” and “TOO LATE” covering walls while camera spins around lone figure
* ambition = skyscraper windows lighting up one by one at night while camera rises upward between buildings
* inner war = same person facing themselves in a dark mirrored room with reflections moving differently from reality
* emptiness = massive luxury apartment with floor-to-ceiling windows overlooking a city skyline, but completely silent and empty
* betrayal = slow-motion handshake turning into shattered glass fragments with dramatic lighting and black background
* healing = cracked ceramic object slowly rebuilding itself piece by piece with warm sunlight breaking through
* chaos = giant spinning wall of news headlines, stock charts, flashing screens, alarms, and city footage collapsing inward
* peace = soft dust particles floating through warm sunlight in a quiet room with sheer curtains moving gently in the wind
* courage = lone figure stepping forward into complete darkness while a faint light source slowly appears ahead
* missed_opportunity = elevator doors closing just before someone reaches them, shown in dramatic slow motion
* overthinking = glowing white lines and diagrams wrapping around a person’s head in darkness like a tangled web
* being_watched = giant realistic eye slowly opening in the shadows of a dark room while camera pushes inward
* losing_control = steering wheel spinning violently on its own in a dark empty car with flashing street lights outside
* silence = loud chaotic scene instantly cutting to total black screen with only one floating dust particle visible
* memory = distorted home video footage projected across walls in a dark room with VHS static and faded colors
mograph.style:
* Motion graphics should feel premium, cinematic, dark, and modern rather than flashy or cartoonish
* Every graphic should look like it belongs in a high-end documentary, luxury commercial, or premium streaming series
* Use smooth animation curves with subtle easing, not stiff linear movement
* Most motion should be slow, deliberate, and confident, with occasional sharp fast cuts for emphasis
* Keep graphics layered with foreground, midground, and background depth to create a 3D feeling
* Use realistic shadows, light leaks, reflections, film grain, noise textures, and subtle blur
* Add depth with parallax movement between text, images, particles, and footage
* Text should never appear flat; use subtle glow, blur, shadow, flicker, or depth effects
* Use kinetic typography for key emotional phrases, but only emphasize the most important words
* Important words should scale larger, shift position, flicker, distort slightly, or animate differently from surrounding text
* Motion graphics should react to the emotion of the scene:

  * fear = unstable movement, flicker, distortion
  * confidence = slow push-in, clean bold typography
  * chaos = overlapping layers, quick cuts, rapid zooms
  * loneliness = minimal movement, empty space, soft fades
  * obsession = repeated loops, fast flashes, stacked layers
* Use split screens, zoom-ins, close crops, overlays, and layered cutouts of faces
* Add subtle animated arrows, circles, underlines, scribbles, grids, and highlights to direct attention
* Graphs, headlines, quotes, screenshots, and interview clips should animate into frame like investigative evidence
* Use smooth camera pushes through 2D layers to simulate a 3D environment
* Add VHS overlays, CRT distortion, scanlines, static bursts, chromatic aberration, and analog noise sparingly
* Use glitch transitions only for dramatic moments, not every scene
* Use speed ramps between clips to make edits feel more dynamic
* Add freeze frames with text overlays for powerful quotes or emotional beats
* Use subtle camera shake for intense scenes like anger, fear, obsession, or confrontation
* Use light leaks, lens flares, dust particles, smoke, fog, and atmospheric overlays to make scenes feel richer
* Animate subtitles dynamically with word-by-word emphasis for the strongest phrases
* Use frame-within-frame layouts like old television screens, camcorders, security footage, or news broadcasts
* Layer multiple media types together:

  * interview clip
  * movie scene
  * stock footage
  * headlines
  * typography
  * particle effects
  * icons or symbols
* Avoid empty space unless it is being used intentionally to create loneliness or tension
* Avoid generic infographic animations or cheap explainer-style graphics
* Avoid bright gradients, playful icons, cartoon effects, or overly colorful motion design
* Avoid overusing transitions; sometimes simple cuts are more powerful
* Use color strategically:

  * red = danger, anger, obsession, power
  * blue = sadness, loneliness, calm
  * yellow = ego, fame, money, attention
  * white = truth, philosophy, reflection
  * black = emptiness, fear, mystery
* Keep fonts bold, cinematic, and minimal
* Use condensed sans serif fonts for powerful statements
* Use serif fonts sparingly for philosophical quotes or emotional lines
* Use large text that fills the frame for major ideas
* Let graphics breathe; not every second needs maximum movement
* Motion graphics should always support the story, not distract from it
* Every animation should make the viewer feel something, not just look impressive
mograph.text_style: One word at a time. Bebas Neue. All caps. White. Appears letter by letter then fades slowly. Never more than 5 words total per mograph.

sound.mood:
The sound design should feel massive, emotional, and atmospheric — like the viewer is floating through someone’s thoughts at 2 AM. The closest reference point is dark orchestral ambient in the style of Hans Zimmer on Interstellar, mixed with lo-fi tape hiss, analog warmth, distant city ambience, and subtle modern sound textures.

The music should not feel like a normal song. It should feel alive. It should feel like it is breathing with the narration.

For the first 60 to 90 seconds:

* No drums
* No heavy percussion
* No aggressive impacts
* No fast-paced rhythm
* Let the soundscape slowly unfold
* Focus on emotional tension, space, and anticipation

The opening should usually begin with:

* low ambient drones
* soft tape hiss
* distant reverb-heavy piano notes
* subtle analog crackle
* deep bass swells
* faint room tone
* soft synth pads that slowly expand
* quiet city ambience, wind, static, or electrical hum

The viewer should feel like they are entering a memory, a dream, or a dark thought.

The music should rise and fall naturally, almost like inhaling and exhaling:

* quiet moment
* slow swell
* slight silence
* deeper bass
* distant choir
* subtle string movement
* another pause
* emotional release

Avoid constant sound. Silence is important. Sometimes removing the music for one second can create more emotional impact than adding another instrument.

When the narration becomes more emotional or philosophical:

* Introduce distant cello
* Add long violin notes with heavy reverb
* Bring in soft choir textures
* Use piano with long sustain and empty space between notes
* Layer in subtle low brass or bass synths underneath
* Add reverse swells before important lines
* Use deep cinematic impacts sparingly for major moments

When the video reaches a turning point:

* Slowly introduce restrained percussion
* Add heartbeat-like bass pulses
* Use deeper string swells
* Increase the intensity of the choir
* Add low cinematic booms and textured impacts
* Let the music feel like it is climbing toward something

For scenes about obsession, fear, or anxiety:

* Use repetitive ticking sounds
* Distorted breathing
* Metallic echoes
* Glitch textures
* Pulsing synths
* Narrow stereo sound
* Tension strings
* Low sub bass that feels uncomfortable
* Ringing tones and distant industrial sounds

For scenes about loneliness or emptiness:

* Minimal piano
* Long pauses
* Soft room reverb
* Wind ambience
* Distant train sounds
* Low drones
* Barely audible choir
* Faint vinyl crackle
* Sparse instrumentation with lots of empty space

For scenes about motivation or rising again:

* Slowly introduce warmer strings
* Use deeper bass with more movement
* Add choir gradually
* Introduce subtle impacts
* Build toward emotional orchestral swells
* Let the music feel bigger, wider, and more hopeful without becoming overly triumphant

The soundtrack should always feel cinematic, premium, and emotional — never like generic YouTube motivation music.

Avoid:

* EDM drops
* Loud trap beats
* Aggressive rap instrumentals
* Overused motivational music
* Bright corporate piano
* Happy ukulele
* Generic stock music
* Cheap “epic trailer” sounds
* Overly dramatic drums too early
* Anything that feels like a gym commercial or entrepreneur montage

The goal is for the music to feel like memory, pressure, ambition, loneliness, nostalgia, and hope all existing at the same time.
sound.ambient: Vinyl crackle, distant city hum, occasional static
sound.silence: Music drops to silence 2 seconds before the most important quote of the video. Holds silence for 3–4 seconds during the quote. Returns as a slow swell after.
sound.swells: First swell at the transformation moment (~60% through). Final swell at the closing philosophical statement. Music ends before the video — last 5 seconds are silence.

editing.edit_pacing:
* Hook section = fastest pacing in the entire video

  * Average cut length: 2–4 seconds
  * Use 1-second cuts occasionally for impact
  * Every shot should introduce a new idea, image, emotion, or contradiction
  * Stack multiple visuals together: interview clip, text overlay, movie scene, city footage, static, close-up, headline
  * Use speed ramps, flash frames, zoom-ins, quick overlays, and glitch cuts
  * Never let the viewer settle too long on one image
  * The goal is to overload curiosity and create tension immediately
  * Hook pacing should feel like the viewer is being pulled into a mystery they need to solve

* Problem section = slightly slower, more controlled pacing

  * Average cut length: 5–8 seconds
  * Allow visuals to breathe slightly more
  * Let interview clips play longer so viewers can connect emotionally
  * Use slow zooms, subtle camera pushes, layered b-roll, and animated text
  * Keep some motion on screen even if the cut stays longer: moving particles, slow push-in, drifting smoke, light flicker
  * This section should feel investigative and immersive, like slowly uncovering the truth behind the problem
  * Alternate between:

    * celebrity interview
    * symbolic b-roll
    * text statement
    * cinematic footage
    * visual metaphor
  * Use pauses intentionally to let important ideas land

* Transformation section = faster again, but more purposeful than the hook

  * Average cut length: 2–3 seconds
  * Increase motion, energy, and visual intensity
  * Use faster pacing to make the turning point feel powerful
  * Show more movement:

    * training clips
    * city time-lapses
    * rising particles
    * hands working
    * clocks moving
    * people running
    * crowds
  * Add more dynamic transitions like:

    * whip pans
    * light leaks
    * flash frames
    * zoom cuts
    * speed ramps
    * glitch distortions
  * Music should also rise here, with more bass, impacts, and subtle percussion
  * This section should feel like momentum, change, and emotional release

* Lesson section = slowest and most reflective pacing

  * Average shot length: 10–15 seconds
  * Sometimes hold one powerful shot for even longer if the emotion is strong enough
  * Use slow camera movement:

    * push-in
    * slow pan
    * slow drift
    * wide static shot
  * Keep fewer cuts and allow the viewer to sit with the message
  * Let the visuals become simpler and cleaner
  * Use:

    * sunrise
    * empty streets
    * city skyline
    * person sitting alone
    * slow rain
    * distant lights
    * close-up on face
  * Subtitles and text should animate more slowly here
  * The ending should feel like exhaling after emotional tension
  * Let the final line sit in silence for 1–2 seconds before cutting away

* Emotional intensity should control pacing

  * If a scene is chaotic, anxious, obsessive, or angry = faster cuts
  * If a scene is lonely, philosophical, reflective, or emotional = slower cuts
  * If a scene is confusing or overwhelming = uneven pacing with sudden cuts and pauses
  * If a scene is inspirational or hopeful = smoother pacing with longer dissolves and wider shots

* Use contrast in pacing

  * Fast sections feel more powerful when they are followed by slow sections
  * Silence feels stronger after loud moments
  * Long shots feel more emotional after fast montages
  * Sudden pauses can be more powerful than another cut
  * Do not make the entire video equally fast or equally slow

* Avoid:

  * keeping the same pacing for too long
  * static visuals with no movement
  * random cuts with no emotional reason
  * over-editing every single second
  * long interview clips with no visual layering
  * fast cutting during emotional lines that need time to breathe
  * slow pacing too early in the video before viewer attention is earned
editing.edit_text_rules: Bold 2–4 word phrases appear every 45–60 seconds. Words appear one at a time. Stay on screen for 2.5 seconds. Never cover the celebrity face. White text on dark, yellow on bright.
editing.edit_end: Last 20 seconds: black screen. One sentence fades in white. Music fades to silence. Channel watermark appears bottom right. No subscribe animation — just presence.
editing.edit_special:
The celebrity's face is NOT always on screen. Their voice is always playing. The face appears only at specific moments. Everything else is B-roll.

Show the celebrity's face when:
▸  They deliver a statement of 5 words or fewer that is punchy, direct, and emotional.
▸  There is a natural pause or breath before a powerful line — the face builds anticipation.
▸  The emotional expression on their face directly amplifies what they are saying.

Cut away to B-roll when:
▸  They are explaining, narrating, or describing something abstract.
▸  A sentence runs longer than 8 seconds.
▸  The visual content of the script demands an image that is stronger than their face.
▸  The celebrity is gesturing broadly or not making direct eye contact.
Cut rhythm:  Every 1.5 – 3 seconds. No shot holds longer than 3 seconds.
First frame:  Must be the celebrity mid-expression or mid-word. No black opening. No fade in. Hard cut directly into the moment.
First 5 seconds:  No title card. No intro. No channel branding. Pure content only.
Audio entry:  Celebrity voice starts at full volume on frame 1. No fade in on the voice.
consecutive celebrity face shots in the hook.
▸  Every cut in the hook should feel like a small surprise — a different angle, a different scale, a different visual register.
▸  Use a SLOW PUSH IN on at least one shot in the hook — a 1.5x zoom over 3 seconds creates subconscious unease and tension.
▸  Bold text overlay appears within the first 8 seconds. 2–4 words maximum. One word at a time.

Emotional target — Hook
The viewer should feel: something is wrong, something is different, I need to understand this. Not yet moved — that comes later. Right now: hooked.
PART 2 — THE PROBLEM  (0:30 TO 40% OF TOTAL RUNTIME)
This is where the video breathes. Slower. Heavier. The celebrity's struggle, loneliness, failure, or fear is established. The viewer should start to recognise themselves in the story.
Zooms:  Very slow push ins only — 1.05x to 1.15x zoom over 8–12 seconds. Creates mounting dread.

▸  At least one moment in the Problem section must have NO music — just the celebrity voice and ambient sound. Minimum 15 seconds of music-free audio.
▸  Use a FREEZE FRAME on the celebrity face at a moment of visible pain or vulnerability. Hold for 1.5 seconds before cutting. This is a punctuation mark.
▸  Pan shots in the Problem section move RIGHT to LEFT only — this is the visual grammar of regression, loss, and going backwards
PART 3 — THE TRANSFORMATION  (40% TO 75% OF TOTAL RUNTIME)
The pivot. The energy shifts. The celebrity finds their obsession, their resilience, their discipline, their defiance. The editing must physically feel like something changing in the viewer's chest.

Pacing — Transformation
Cut rhythm:  Starts at 6–8 seconds per shot, accelerates to 2–3 seconds by the peak moment.
Music:  Begins building from –14dB toward –8dB under voice. Introduces drums or rhythmic element if present.
Transition type:  Switch from cross dissolves and slow cuts to hard cuts and glitch transitions.▸  The PEAK of the transformation — the most powerful quote of the section — must have: music swell to its loudest point, a hard cut, a bold text overlay, and a slow push in all happening simultaneously.
▸  Pan shots in the Transformation section move LEFT to RIGHT — forward, progress, ascension.
▸  Use a FAST MONTAGE of 4–6 clips, each held for 1–1.5 seconds, at the emotional peak. This is the heartbeat moment.
▸  At least one MOGRAPH B-ROLL fires in the Transformation section — triggered by the peak keyword (rising, fire, pressure, obsession).
PART 4 — THE LESSON  (75% TO END)
The resolution. Philosophical. Reflective. Slow. The viewer should feel something they cannot name — a mixture of inspiration, melancholy, and clarity. This section should feel like the end of a great film.

Pacing — Lesson
Cut rhythm:  Every 10–18 seconds. The slowest cuts in the entire video.
Music:  Fades back down to –16dB. Melodic only — no rhythm. Feels like the music is thinking.

▸  The final quote of the video should be displayed as on-screen text on a black or near-black background. White. Slow fade in. No animation — just appearance.
▸  Do NOT end with a subscribe call to action over the emotional moment. CTA comes only after the emotional end — a separate 5-second card.
▸  The last frame before the end card must hold for at least 3 seconds in silence.
CUTS & TRANSITIONS


Every transition type has a meaning. Use the correct transition for the correct emotional context. Using the wrong transition is the same as using the wrong word in a sentence.

HARD CUT
The default. No transition effect. One frame ends, the next begins. Clean, direct, and strong. Used when the edit should feel invisible or when the energy is high.
Use when:  Fast pacing, hook section, transformation peak, cutting between two high-energy clips.
Do not use when:  Moving between two emotionally heavy moments — the abruptness will feel cold, not powerful.
Sound design:  No transition sound. The cut is the sound.

▸  Hard cuts on the beat of the music when music has a rhythmic element. The cut lands on the kick drum or snare — not before it.

VHS GLITCH CUT
A 4–8 frame digital distortion that creates a momentary break in reality before the new clip begins. Signals: disruption, contradiction, an uncomfortable truth arriving.
Duration:  4–8 frames (0.13–0.26 seconds at 30fps). Never longer.
Use when:  Cutting from the celebrity saying something comfortable to footage that contradicts it. Cutting between two clashing emotional states. Beginning of the transformation section.
Do not use when:  The emotional register of both clips is the same. Never in the Lesson section.
Sound design:  A 0.1-second burst of white static noise (–12dB) synced exactly to the first glitch frame. Cuts to silence instantly on the new clip.

▸  The VHS glitch cut fires a maximum of 4 times per video. Overuse destroys its impact.
▸  Always follow a VHS glitch cut with a hold — the next shot sits for at least 3 seconds without a cut. The glitch was the shock; now let the viewer absorb the new image.

FILM BURN TRANSITION
An organic, warm flash of amber or white light that briefly overexposes the frame before the new clip appears. Signals: time passing, memory, the past giving way to the present.
Duration:  10–18 frames (0.33–0.6 seconds at 30fps).
Use when:  Moving between two different time periods in the celebrity's life. Moving from the Problem section into the Transformation section. Transitioning from one celebrity to another within the same video.
Do not use when:  High energy fast-cut sequences. The hook. The transformation peak montage.
Sound design:  A 0.3-second low vinyl crackle sound (–16dB) that starts 2 frames before the light flash and fades out as the new clip appears. Optional: very brief tape hiss underneath.

▸  The colour temperature of the film burn matches the emotional tone — warm amber for nostalgia and memory, cold white for clarity and revelation.

CROSS DISSOLVE
One image fades into another over a set duration. Signals: gentle passage of time, emotional continuity, two ideas that are related and flow into each other.
Duration:  18–24 frames (0.6–0.8 seconds at 30fps). Never shorter — a fast dissolve looks cheap.
Use when:  Problem section slow pacing. Moving between two B-roll clips of the same emotional register. Any transition into the Lesson section.
Do not use when:  The hook. Fast-cut sequences. Transformation peak. Celebrity face to B-roll — always use a hard cut for these.
Sound design:  No added sound. The audio crossfade is natural.

SMASH CUT
An abrupt, jarring cut from a quiet or slow moment into something loud or fast — or vice versa. Signals: a sudden shift in reality, a contradiction revealed, a truth landing.
Use when:  The most powerful line of the entire video. A confession after a long silence. A moment where the celebrity says something that reframes everything before it.
Do not use when:  Anywhere that has not been built up to with at least 10 seconds of slow or quiet content.
Sound design:  The audio of the new clip hits at full volume on frame 1. No fade in. The sonic impact IS the transition.

⚠️  DISCIPLINE RULE  Use the smash cut once per video maximum. It is the most powerful editorial tool and loses all power through repetition.

SLOW FADE TO BLACK
The image gradually fades to complete black over a set duration. Signals: finality, weight, the end of a chapter. The visual equivalent of silence.
Duration:  24–40 frames (0.8–1.3 seconds at 30fps).
Use when:  End of the video before the CTA card. End of the Problem section into a new thought. When a quote needs its weight honoured before moving on.
Sound design:  Music or ambient sound continues through the fade and holds on the black frame before the next clip begins.

WHIP PAN
An extremely fast camera pan that blurs the frame before landing on the next clip. Signals: urgency, physical energy, forward momentum.
Duration:  6–10 frames.
Use when:  Transformation section only. Fast montage sequences. Cutting between two clips of the same scene at different angles.
Do not use when:  Philosophical or emotional content. The Lesson section. Any moment requiring stillness.
Sound design:  A short whoosh sound (–10dB) that peaks at the middle frame of the blur and cuts to silence on landing.
 
SOUND DESIGN


Sound is 50% of the emotional experience. The music, the ambient layers, the transition sounds, the silence — every audio decision is as important as the visual decision. A bad sound choice destroys a great visual.

BACKGROUND MUSIC ARCHITECTURE
The music is not a soundtrack playing behind a video. It is a breathing, living layer that responds to the emotional content in real time. It must feel like it knows what is being said.

Music Volume Rules
Celebrity voice reference:  0 dB (never touched)
Music — Lesson section:  –18 dB to –20 dB below voice. Almost subliminal.
Music — Problem section:  –16 dB to –18 dB. Present but never intrusive.
Music — Hook:  –14 dB to –16 dB. The viewer is aware of it.
Music — Transformation build:  –12 dB to –8 dB. Music asserts itself.
Music — Transformation peak:  –6 dB to –4 dB. Music and voice share the emotional space equally.
Music — Final 30 seconds:  Slow fade from –16 dB to complete silence (–60 dB) over 20–25 seconds.

Music Behaviour Rules
▸  Music NEVER starts on beat 1 of a track. Always enter 2–4 bars in — as if the music was already playing and the video joined it mid-thought.
▸  Music does NOT restart between sections. It is one continuous piece that evolves. If the track changes, use a cross-fade of minimum 8 seconds between tracks — the join must be inaudible.
▸  If the music has a clear melodic hook or chorus, that moment should be held in reserve for the transformation peak. Do not expose the most recognisable musical moment early.
▸  Preferred musical register: dark ambient, cinematic orchestral, lo-fi tape-saturated. Think: Hans Zimmer, Ólafur Arnalds, Brian Eno, Hammock. No obvious beats in the hook or problem sections.

THE SILENCE RULE
Silence is the most powerful audio tool available. It must be used deliberately, not accidentally.

🔇  THE SILENCE MOMENT  Every Dopamine Loop video must contain at least one moment of complete silence — no music, no ambient sound — lasting a minimum of 3 seconds and a maximum of 6 seconds. This moment is planned, not accidental. It falls immediately before or after the most important quote in the video.

▸  To execute: fade music to –60dB (complete silence) 1.5 seconds before the silence moment begins. Hold for 3–6 seconds. Re-enter with music at –20dB using a very slow 4-second fade in.
▸  During the silence, the only sound is the celebrity's voice. If the celebrity also pauses naturally — hold that silence. Do not fill it.
▸  The silence must not be preceded by a loud music moment. Build down over at least 10 seconds before the drop to silence.

AMBIENT SOUND LAYERS
Under the music, at all times, there is a subtle ambient layer that creates the physical feeling of being in a space. This is mixed so low the viewer never consciously hears it — they only feel the space.

Problem section ambient:  Distant city hum, low wind, or faint crowd noise. –28dB to –32dB. Just enough to prevent the silence from feeling clinical.
Hook ambient:  Very low vinyl crackle or tape hiss. –30dB. Adds texture and warmth to the sound.
Transformation ambient:  Fades out completely as the music rises. The ambient world disappears as the internal world takes over.
Lesson ambient:  Returns. Very quiet outdoor sound — wind, distant room tone. –30dB. The world comes back slowly.

TRANSITION SOUND DESIGN
Every transition type has a specific sound signature. These sounds are mixed low — they should be felt more than heard.

TRANSITION	SOUND	NOTES
Hard Cut	No sound added	The cut is invisible — the audio does the work
VHS Glitch Cut	0.1 sec white static burst at –12dB	Synced to first glitch frame, cuts instantly to silence
Film Burn	Low vinyl crackle 0.3 sec at –16dB	Starts 2 frames before the flash, fades as new clip opens
Cross Dissolve	No added sound	Natural audio crossfade only
Smash Cut	No added sound	New clip audio hits at full volume — that IS the sound
Slow Fade to Black	Very faint low hum –28dB	Fades with the image, gone before next clip
Whip Pan	Short whoosh at –10dB	Peaks at middle of blur, cuts to silence on landing
Freeze Frame	Subtle low hum –20dB	Enters on freeze, fades over duration of hold

AUDIO SWELL TRIGGERS
An audio swell is a planned, deliberate increase in music volume that creates an emotional crescendo. There are exactly two swells per video.

▸  SWELL 1: The Transformation Peak. Music rises from its current level to –4dB over 8–12 seconds. Holds at peak for 4–6 seconds. Then drops back to –12dB over 4 seconds.
▸  SWELL 2: The Final Quote. Music rises from its current level to –8dB over 6–8 seconds. Then immediately begins the long fade to silence that ends the video.
▸  Swells NEVER begin on a beat or bar boundary — they start 1–2 seconds before the bar line. This makes them feel organic, not mechanical.
▸  There must be at least 3 minutes of video between Swell 1 and Swell 2. If the video is shorter than 8 minutes, Swell 1 may be removed and only Swell 2 used.
 The colour grade defines the emotional temperature of every frame. Dopamine Loop has one consistent grade with section-specific modifications. The grade never changes between videos — it is the channel's visual fingerprint.

THE MASTER GRADE — APPLIED TO ALL FOOTAGE
Every clip in every video starts with this base grade before any section-specific adjustments are made.

Exposure & Contrast
Exposure:  –0.3 stops. Slightly underexposed. The world is darker than it appears.
Contrast:  +25. Increases the separation between darks and lights.
Highlights:  –35. Pulls the bright areas down — prevents the image from feeling bright or cheerful.
Shadows:  +15. Lifts the blacks slightly — creates a film-like quality, prevents pure crushed blacks.
Whites:  –20. Keeps whites from clipping.
Blacks:  –10. Keeps the shadows rich and present.

Colour — Hue Adjustments
Temperature:  –8 (cooler). The overall image shifts slightly blue and cold.
Tint:  +4 (slight green push). Adds a faint sickly quality — the world is imperfect.
Saturation:  –25. Significant desaturation. Colours exist but are muted — they do not compete with emotion.
Vibrance:  +10. Slightly boosts mid-tone colours without touching the already-saturated areas. Skin tones retain some life.

Tone Curve
Shadows (0–30):  Slightly lifted S-curve — raise the bottom of the curve 5–8 points. Prevents total black. Film look.
Midtones (30–70):  Slight dip — pull the middle of the curve down 3–5 points. Darkens the midrange for a heavier feel.
Highlights (70–100):  Pull down 8–10 points. Prevents hot spots on faces and windows.

HSL — Colour-Specific Adjustments
Reds — Saturation:  –15. Skin tones become slightly pallid. Intentional — warmth is reduced.
Oranges — Saturation:  –20. Further reduces the warmth of skin.
Blues — Saturation:  +10. Blues are the one colour that retains presence — cold, sky, loneliness.
Yellows — Luminance:  +5. Very slight lift to yellows — creates the faint amber warmth in shadow areas.

Film Grain
Grain amount:  18–22. Visible but not distracting. Medium grain size.
Grain size:  28–32. Slightly larger than digital noise — feels organic and analog.
Roughness:  55. Irregular grain structure — not uniform digital noise.

🎞️  THE RESULT  The master grade produces an image that feels: slightly cold, slightly heavy, slightly aged. Colours exist but do not shout. The world feels real but weighted. Every frame should feel like it could be a film still from a serious drama made in the 1990s.

SECTION-SPECIFIC GRADE MODIFICATIONS
Applied ON TOP of the master grade. These push the emotional temperature of each section.

Hook — Grade Modification
Contrast:  Additional +10 on top of master. Higher punch.
Shadows:  Additional –5. Slightly darker shadows in the hook.
Film grain:  Grain amount increases to 28–32. More textured and raw.

Problem Section — Grade Modification
Temperature:  Additional –5 cooler. The cold increases.
Saturation:  Additional –10. Even more drained.
Vignette:  Add a subtle vignette — darken corners by 15–20%. Viewer feels closed in.
Film grain:  Grain roughness increases to 65–70. The image feels more unstable.

Transformation Section — Grade Modification
Temperature:  Gradually shift from –8 toward 0 (neutral) over the section. The cold lifts.
Saturation:  Gradually shift from –35 toward –20 over the section. Colour returns slowly.
Contrast:  Additional +5. Image gets sharper as the energy rises.
Vignette:  Vignette reduces to zero by the transformation peak. The walls open up.

Lesson Section — Grade Modification
Temperature:  Settles at –4 to –6. Slightly cool but warmer than the Problem section.
Saturation:  Settles at –20 to –25. A little colour has returned. Not much.
Shadows:  +5 on top of master. Slightly lifted blacks — a gentler feeling.
Film grain:  Grain amount reduces to 12–15. The image is calmer and cleaner.
Vignette:  A very subtle, soft vignette returns — not oppressive, just intimate. 8–10% corner darkening.

VHS & CRT OVERLAY INSTRUCTIONS
Applied as a composited overlay layer on top of the colour-graded footage at all times.

VHS noise overlay opacity:  8–12%. Barely visible. Felt as texture, not seen as an effect.
CRT scanline overlay opacity:  4–6%. At this level, the lines appear only when the image is bright.
Letterbox bars:  Always on. Top and bottom black bars. 2.39:1 aspect ratio crop. Height of each bar: 12% of frame height.
Light leak overlay:  Applied once per video — at the transition into the Transformation section. Opacity: 30–40%. Duration: 8–12 frames. Warm amber colour only.
Dust particles:  Floating particle layer at 6–8% opacity throughout. Very slow movement — particles drift, they do not fall fast.

▸  VHS noise and CRT scanlines are composite layers, not filters on individual clips. They sit above everything and apply uniformly.
▸  On the FREEZE FRAME moment: increase VHS noise opacity to 20–25% briefly — the image becomes slightly less stable as time stops.
▸  During the SILENCE MOMENT: reduce VHS noise to near zero (2–3%). The texture disappears and the image becomes strangely clean — this subconsciously signals that something important is happening.
 
CHAPTER 7
ON-SCREEN TEXT & MOTION GRAPHICS


Text is not decoration. Every word that appears on screen is a deliberate editorial choice that amplifies, punctuates, or reframes what the celebrity is saying. Text should feel like a thought entering the viewer's mind, not a caption.

ON-SCREEN TEXT RULES

Font:  Bebas Neue — all caps, no exceptions.
Colour:  White by default. Yellow (#D4B800) for emphasis or danger. Red (#B03030) for the most intense statements.
Size:  Large. Text should occupy 15–25% of the frame width per word. Not a subtitle — a statement.
Position:  Lower third by default. Centre frame for the most impactful quotes. Never overlapping the celebrity's face or eyes.
Frequency:  One text event every 40–60 seconds. Not more. Text loses power through overuse.
Maximum words per event:  4 words. Fewer is almost always better.

Text Animation — Appearance
▸  Words appear ONE AT A TIME. Each word fades in or snaps in over 3–6 frames. The gap between each word appearing is 0.3–0.5 seconds.
▸  After all words are on screen, they hold for 1.5–2.5 seconds depending on word count (fewer words = longer hold).
▸  Exit: words fade out together over 8–12 frames. Or: words remain until the cut — they cut with the frame.

Special Text Effects
Glitch text:  For the most intense or contradictory statements. Letters briefly scramble (2–3 frames) before settling into the correct word. Used maximum 2 times per video.
Burn away:  Text appears, holds, then disintegrates from left to right over 12–18 frames. Used for the final quote of the video only.
Shake text:  Letters vibrate subtly (1–2 pixel offset, 4 frames of animation) for words like FEAR, NOTHING, EVERYTHING, ALONE. Adds physical sensation to the word.

MOGRAPH B-ROLL TRIGGER RULES
When no real clip exists for a moment in the script, a 3-second motion graphic B-roll fires. The AI reads the script, identifies the trigger keyword, selects the matching mograph, and inserts it. These are the rules for that process.

Minimum time between mographs:  90 seconds. No two mographs back-to-back or close together.
Maximum mographs per video:  5. More than this makes the video feel like it has no real footage.
Duration:  3 seconds exactly. The mograph cuts out on beat 3.
Audio during mograph:  Celebrity voice continues uninterrupted over the mograph. The mograph is visual only.
Music during mograph:  Music continues. Consider a subtle audio swell of +2dB for the 3 seconds of the mograph and –2dB return after. Makes the mograph feel musically supported.

Mograph–to–Clip Transition
▸  The shot BEFORE the mograph: always a hard cut into the mograph.
▸  The shot AFTER the mograph: always a hard cut out of the mograph. Never dissolve in or out of a mograph — the hard edges emphasise its deliberate, graphic nature.

SUBTITLE / CAPTION STYLE
Subtitles are present throughout the video for accessibility and silent viewing. They are styled to be readable without being distracting.
Font:  DM Sans or Arial. Clean and neutral — in contrast to the display text.
Size:  Smaller than on-screen emphasis text. Occupies subtitle zone only.
Position:  Bottom of frame, inside the letterbox bars. Never above the celebrity face.
Colour:  White with a subtle dark drop shadow. No background box — the text floats cleanly.
Word highlighting:  The currently spoken word is displayed in yellow or slightly brighter white. Previous words remain in standard white. This creates a follow-along effect.
Line breaks:  Break at natural speech pauses. Maximum 7 words per subtitle line.
 
THE QUALITY CHECKLIST


Before any video is exported, run through this checklist. Every item must be confirmed. A video that fails any of these checks is not ready for upload.

AUDIO CHECKS
▸  Celebrity voice is audible and clear throughout the entire video — including under music.
▸  Music never exceeds the voice at any point.
▸  The silence moment exists and lasts at least 3 seconds.
▸  Transition sounds are present and mixed correctly (not too loud, not absent).
▸  The final 20 seconds have music fading to complete silence.
▸  No abrupt audio cuts — every audio edit is either intentional (smash cut) or smooth.

VISUAL CHECKS
▸  The master colour grade is applied to every clip.
▸  VHS noise and CRT scanline overlays are present and at correct opacity.
▸  Letterboxing is present on all long-form content.
▸  Film grain is present and consistent throughout.
▸  No clip runs without any camera movement for longer than 8 seconds (push in, pan, or zoom must be active).
▸  Celebrity face appears in 30–40% of total runtime (use a rough edit review to estimate).
▸  No filler shots — every B-roll choice is intentional.

PACING CHECKS
▸  The hook contains no shot longer than 3 seconds.
▸  The Lesson section contains no shot shorter than 8 seconds.
▸  The silence moment does not have a cut within 5 seconds of it beginning.
▸  The Transformation peak has: music swell + hard cut + text overlay + push in — all simultaneous.
▸  The final frame holds for at least 3 seconds before the end card.

TEXT & GRAPHICS CHECKS
▸  No more than 5 mograph B-roll insertions total.
▸  Minimum 90 seconds between consecutive mographs.
▸  On-screen text events occur no more than once every 40 seconds.
▸  No text overlaps or obscures the celebrity's face or eyes.
▸  All on-screen text is in Bebas Neue, all caps.
▸  Subtitles are present, correctly timed, and styled per the subtitle rules.



THE GOAL IS SIMPLE.
Every Dopamine Loop video should feel like a short film about a human being.
Not a compilation. Not a montage. Not a motivational reel.
A film. About a human being. That another human being cannot stop watching.

shorts.formula:
FUNDAMENTAL DIFFERENCES

ELEMENT	LONG-FORM	SHORTS
Aspect ratio	16:9 horizontal	9:16 vertical
Duration	8–18 minutes	30–60 seconds
Hook time	30 seconds	0 seconds — starts immediately
Intro sequence	Yes — 8 seconds	No — never
Celebrity face time	30–40% of runtime	50–65% of runtime
Music volume	Mixed low under voice	Slightly louder — shares space with voice
Captions	Bottom subtitle zone	Centre frame, word by word, bold
Letterboxing	Always on	Never — full vertical frame
Film grain	18–22 amount	10–14 amount — slightly cleaner
Colour grade	Master grade applied	Master grade + +5 saturation (slightly more vivid)

SHORTS — STRUCTURE
Every Short follows a precise 5-part structure. No deviations.

▸  SECONDS 0–3: The celebrity mid-sentence or mid-expression. No context. The viewer is dropped directly into a moment of maximum intensity. This is the only hook you have.
▸  SECONDS 3–10: The setup — 1–2 lines of the celebrity establishing the subject. B-roll may appear here if it directly illustrates the words. One text overlay maximum.
▸  SECONDS 10–40: The core content — the main quote, confession, or story. Celebrity face alternating with tight, fast B-roll. Captions always visible. Music builds slowly.
▸  SECONDS 40–55: The peak and the revelation. The most powerful line. On-screen text. Music at its highest point for the Short.
▸  SECONDS 55–60: Silence or near-silence. One final word or phrase on screen. Fade to black. No CTA in this window — let the ending breathe.

SHORTS — CAPTION STYLE
Captions in Shorts are a primary visual element, not an accessibility feature. They are designed to be read, felt, and shared.
Position:  Centred horizontally. Vertically positioned in the bottom third — roughly 65–75% down the frame.
Font:  Bebas Neue. All caps. Bold.
Size:  Large — each word occupies approximately 8–10% of frame width.
Animation:  Word by word. Each word snaps in (2 frame fade) as it is spoken. The spoken word is white. Previous words dim to 60% opacity immediately.
Background:  No background box. No outline. A subtle text shadow (2–3px, 40% black) only.
Colour treatment:  Key emotional words (pain, nothing, alone, obsession, cost) flash yellow for 3 frames on appearance before settling to white.

SHORTS — SOUND DESIGN
Music level:  –10dB to –8dB under voice. Slightly louder than long-form — the Short format expects more energy.
Transition sounds:  Use short whoosh sounds (–8dB) on every cut between B-roll clips. Shorts move fast — transition sounds fill the space.
Bass boost:  Increase sub-bass of music by +3dB below 80Hz. Creates physical presence on phone speakers.
Voice treatment:  Slight compression on voice (+2dB make-up gain). Voice feels more present and immediate in the vertical format.
Silence:  One moment of complete silence in the Short — the last 2–3 seconds before fade to black. Mandatory.
shorts.hook:
Shorts Hook Rule

The first 1–2 seconds must immediately create tension, confusion, or emotional curiosity
Open with the strongest line, not context
Start with a contradiction, painful truth, celebrity quote, or emotionally charged statement
Use large subtitles immediately on screen
Use movement instantly: zoom, glitch, fast cut, dramatic close-up, text pop-in
Never begin with “Have you ever…” or slow introductions
First frame should visually feel important
Use a hook formula like:
“The people everyone admires are usually the most miserable.”
“Success can destroy the exact person who wanted it.”
“Nobody tells men this until it is too late.”
“He got everything he wanted… and still felt empty.”
The first 3 seconds should make the viewer feel like they are missing an important truth if they scroll away
shorts.visuals:
Shorts Visual Style

Fast-paced editing with cuts every 1–2 seconds
Large cinematic subtitles in the center or lower center of frame
Heavy use of close-ups, facial expressions, emotional moments, and layered b-roll
Mix celebrity clips, city footage, dark rooms, lonely streets, clocks, money, interviews, text overlays, and symbolic visuals
Add subtle VHS overlays, film grain, tape hiss visuals, scanlines, and light glitch effects
Use vertical split screens and stacked footage to maximize visual density
Important words should appear larger than the rest
Use red for anger, blue for loneliness, yellow for ego or fame, white for philosophical lines
Always keep movement on screen: particles, zooms, camera pushes, subtitles animating, smoke, flicker, overlays
Shorts should feel intense, emotional, premium, and addictive
shorts.music: short use of trending audio that matches the emotion of the video but well edited with sound reductions and silence during the hard hitting statements

thumbnail.thumb_concept:
Thumbnail Concept Rule

Use one celebrity face as the focal point
Face should show strong emotion:
sadness
anger
emptiness
intensity
reflection
fear
loneliness
Style should look like high-end paper collage art mixed with cinematic digital painting
Use soft layered paper textures, torn edges, shadows, grain, faded brush strokes, and subtle lighting
Background should be minimal and dark with one symbolic object
Every thumbnail should use a different soft color palette depending on emotion:
blue-grey = loneliness
dark red = anger, obsession
faded yellow = fame, ego
black and white = emptiness, philosophy
muted green = envy, sickness, corruption
pale orange = nostalgia, childhood
soft purple = mystery, identity
Add subtle texture so the thumbnail feels handmade and artistic rather than generic
Keep the composition simple and readable even at small size
thumbnail.thumb_text_formula:
Text Font Style on Thumbnail

Use bold condensed sans serif fonts
Fonts should feel cinematic, premium, minimal, and emotionally heavy
Best style references:
condensed all caps
bold block text
slightly distressed or textured edges
minimal serif font for rare philosophical lines
Text should usually be white, off-white, faded yellow, or red
Add slight shadow, grain, or glow for separation
Keep text large and easy to read on mobile
Usually use 2–5 words maximum
thumbnail.thumb_title_formula:
Title Formula

Why [emotion/problem] is destroying you
The hidden cost of [success/fame/attention]
Why [celebrity/person] was never truly happy
The truth about [loneliness/discipline/modern life]
The problem with being [liked/successful/normal]
How [emotion/fear] quietly ruins people
Why everyone feels [lost/empty/tired]
The dark side of [success/social media/fame]
What nobody tells you about [men/loneliness/discipline]
The price of refusing to be normal
Why [celebrity] felt empty after success
The pain of becoming yourself
Stop wasting your potential
Why modern life feels meaningless
Why being different hurts
He got everything and still felt empty
The reason you still feel lost
Why nobody understands you
Success will not save you
The truth about men and loneliness
thumbnail.thumb_forbidden: Forbidden Thumbnail Elements  No generic smiling faces No bright colorful backgrounds No exaggerated YouTube reaction faces No arrows, circles, or clickbait clutter No more than one main face unless absolutely necessary No tiny unreadable text No stock-photo-looking images No meme faces or childish expressions No bright neon unless it matches nightlife or temptation theme No overused luxury imagery like random cars, cash, or mansions with no emotional context No busy background with too many objects No overly polished AI faces No fake-looking hands or distorted anatomy No corporate or “motivational guru” style thumbnails No overuse of red glowing eyes or cliché “evil” edits No more than 2–5 words of text No visual clutter that distracts from the main emotion

seo.description:
SEO Package Template Rules

SEO title should combine emotional curiosity with searchable keywords
Main keyword should appear early in the title
Description should repeat the core topic naturally within the first 2 lines
Use celebrity names when relevant for searchability
Include words like:
motivation
loneliness
masculinity
self improvement
psychology
success
discipline
philosophy
mental health
modern life
Use 3–8 hashtags maximum
Use a mix of broad and niche tags
Add tags related to:
celebrity names
topic
emotion
philosophy
self-improvement
masculinity
motivation
Titles should be under 70 characters if possible
Descriptions should be emotionally written, not robotic
Always include a CTA near the end of the description
seo.tags: motivation, philosophy, self improvement, mindset, discipline, ambition, success, celebrity wisdom, kobe bryant, steve jobs
seo.cta: Description CTA  “If this video spoke to you, subscribe for more cinematic videos about modern life, loneliness, success, masculinity, and self-worth.” “Comment your thoughts below: what part of this video hit you the hardest?” “Subscribe to Dopamine Loop for more dark philosophical storytelling and cinematic self-improvement content.” “Watch the next video if you want to understand why modern life feels so empty.
